source: Similar to hemoglobin, a low hematocrit can indicate anemia associated with RA.
Anemia is frequently observed in RA, particularly anemia of chronic disease, which can be detected by a low hemoglobin level.
Low mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) can also be associated with microcytic anemia in RA.
Low mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) can be seen in microcytic anemia in RA.
Microcytosis (low MCV) can be seen in RA, particularly in the context of anemia of chronic disease.
Thrombocytosis is sometimes observed in RA, potentially due to inflammation or other factors.
Low red blood cell count can indicate anemia associated with RA.
Elevated RDW is a common finding in RA, reflecting increased red blood cell variation in size, often due to chronic inflammation.
Elevated white blood cell count can be seen in RA, reflecting inflammation and immune activation.
